CLARITY.justification: The paper has the standard high-level organization (intro/method/experiments/conclusion) and the proposed contributions (globally optimized neural model, new LSTM/segment/syntactic features) are stated, but the presentation is severely harmed by extensive repetition, LaTeX/artifact noise and formatting errors (many duplicated paragraphs in Related Work and elsewhere). Notation is uneven: Equation 1 is referenced but not shown in the extract and some symbols/terms are introduced without clear definition; figures/tables are referenced (e.g. Figure 4/5, Table 1/4/5/6) but are not presented here and descriptions are sometimes terse. Overall the writing contains many repeated sentences, broken lines and grammatical/formatting issues that make important sections hard to follow and require substantial cleanup.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The experiments are reasonably comprehensive: evaluation on two standard benchmarks (ACE05 and CONLL04), development analyses (Sec. 3.3) comparing local vs. global training and beam-size tradeoffs, and final comparisons to prior work (Table 6). Ablation/sensitivity evidence is provided (e.g., syntactic-feature ablation with significance testing in Sec. 3.3.3, scheduled sampling and beam-size studies in Sec. 3.3.2). Related work coverage is extensive (Sec. 4) and many training details for reproduction are given (optimizer, learning rate, clipping, l2, pretraining schedule, dataset splits and glove link), though some implementation specifics (complete hyperparameter/table contents, random seeds, or released code) are not included. The paper does not discuss failure cases or limitations explicitly, so overall I rate it solid but with minor gaps.

IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles an important problem—end-to-end joint entity and relation extraction—and demonstrates clear empirical gains: it reports state-of-the-art results with improvements of +1.9% on ACE05 and +6.8% on CONLL04. By adapting globally normalized/beam-search training to neural table-filling for relation extraction (and integrating parser encoder hidden layers and segmental LSTM features), it provides a convincing case that global optimization benefits neural structured prediction and offers techniques that are broadly applicable. These results are likely to influence subsequent work on neural structured IE and related sequence/structure prediction tasks, though the contribution is an important advance rather than a paradigm shift.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper's main claims (that a globally optimized neural table-filling model with LSTM/segmental features and parser-hidden syntactic features improves end-to-end relation extraction) are stated and supported by experiments (development ablations in Sect. 3.3, significance test for syntactic features reported in Table 5, and final test improvements reported in Table 6). The mathematical/formulation parts are standard and correct (e.g. cross-entropy loss Eq. (2), beam/global training following Andor et al. with early-update described in Sect. 2.4 and Algorithm 1). The experimental methodology is generally sound (ACE05/CONLL04 benchmarks, comparisons to M&B2016/L&J2014/M&S2014), but some implementation/training details for global training (beam training schedule, parser pretraining/domain alignment) and significance of final test gains are not fully reported, so there are minor gaps that do not overturn the main conclusions.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares to strong, relevant baselines (Miwa & Bansal 2016, Li & Ji 2014, Miwa & Sasaki 2014) and reports results on standard splits of ACE05 and CONLL04 using the same micro-F1 metric (see "We follow Li and Ji (2014) and Miwa and Bansal (2016)" and "Table 6 shows the final results..."). It also includes useful ablations/development experiments (local vs global, syntactic features with p<10^-4) and analyzes beam size and training strategies (Tables 4–5 and the Development Experiments section). Minor omissions remain: not all recent competing neural end-to-end systems discussed (e.g., Gupta et al. 2016 is mentioned but not clearly reported in final comparison), so coverage is good but not fully comprehensive.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: This is squarely within top NLP/ML venues: it addresses end-to-end relation extraction, a central NLP/information-extraction task, and builds on recent neural structured-prediction work (e.g., Miwa & Bansal 2016; Andor et al. 2016; Wiseman & Rush 2016). The formulation (table-filling joint entity+relation), methods (bi‑LSTM features, segmental representations, parser-hidden syntactic features, globally-normalized beam training) and evaluation (ACE05 and CONLL04 benchmarks, micro F1, statistical tests) match community standards. The paper is positioned with appropriate prior work and baselines and thus is an excellent fit for a top-tier NLP/ML conference.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper primarily combines existing techniques rather than proposing a wholly new paradigm: it applies globally-normalized neural training (beam search + early-update, following Andor et al. and Wiseman & Rush) to an end-to-end table-filling neural relation-extraction framework (following Miwa & Bansal), and augments representations with parser encoder hidden layers (Dozat & Manning) and LSTM-Minus segment features. The authors do claim novelty in being the first to globally optimize neural end-to-end RE and clearly differentiate from prior work, but the methods are non-trivial engineering integrations of known components, so the contribution is incremental/moderately novel rather than highly original.
